title: Conditioning and protecting a leather belt
subtitle: Clean, condition, and wax a leather belt so it stays soft and water-resistant
excerpt: How to clean a worn leather belt, work in conditioner, and finish with a wax to add water resistance. Covers identifying the leather grade, the right cleaner for the belt, and the buffing step that brings up a soft sheen.

glossaryTerms.definition: Leather that keeps the natural surface of the hide, including marks and grain pattern. The strongest and most durable grade; takes conditioner well and develops a patina with use.
A blend of beeswax, paraffin, and oils worked into leather to add water resistance and a soft surface sheen. Applied after conditioning, never instead of it.

content.text: A leather belt should last 20 years if it gets a clean and condition twice a year. A worn-looking belt usually has plenty of life left; the surface is dry and dusty rather than damaged. The fix is a 20-minute care session.
Step 1, identify the leather
Look at the surface of the belt under good light. A
full-grain leather

belt shows natural grain pattern and small marks. A corrected-grain or split-leather belt has a smooth surface with a printed pattern. Both take cleaner and conditioner; only full-grain takes wax to a soft sheen.
Step 2, wipe off surface dust

Wipe the belt with a soft dry cloth from buckle end to tail. Run a fingernail under the buckle pin to lift any grit that has gathered. Surface dust scratches the leather during cleaning if it is not lifted first.
Step 3, apply leather cleaner

Squeeze a small amount of leather cleaner onto a clean cloth. Wipe along the belt in long strokes. The cloth picks up grey or brown dirt; turn the cloth often. Avoid household cleaners; soap and detergents strip natural oils.
Step 4, allow to dry
Hang the belt over a clean towel rail in a dry room. Allow at least an hour to dry naturally. Heat from a radiator or hair dryer dries the leather too fast and causes cracking.
Step 5, work in conditioner

Apply a small amount of leather conditioner to a fresh cloth. Rub into the belt in small circles, working the conditioner along the whole length. The leather will darken; the colour returns as it dries. Get the conditioner into the holes around the buckle pin position and the keeper loop. Leave the belt for an hour to absorb.
Step 6, apply leather wax
Rub a small amount of
leather wax
onto a clean cloth. Apply along the belt in long strokes. The wax sits on the surface and forms a thin film. Leave for 15 minutes to set.
Step 7, buff to a soft sheen
Buff with a clean dry cloth using long firm strokes. The wax film levels out and brings up a soft sheen. The belt is now clean, fed, and water-resistant.
Variations

On a heavy bridle leather belt, the wax step can be replaced with neatsfoot oil for a deeper feed. On a patent leather belt, skip conditioner and wax; use only a damp cloth and a polish made for patent leather. On a suede belt, use a suede brush and a suede cleaner; the methods in this tutorial damage suede.
Care

Twice a year is enough for a belt in everyday use. Wipe spills with a dry cloth at once. Store the belt rolled, not folded sharply; a sharp fold creates a crease that does not come out. Keep the belt out of direct sunlight.
Troubleshooter

attrs.symptom: Wax leaves a white film instead of a shine
attrs.cause: Too much wax in one pass, or the buffing pass was too soft.
attrs.fix: Buff harder with a clean dry cloth. If the film persists, wipe with a fresh dry cloth and re-buff.

attrs.cause: Conditioner was applied unevenly, or the leather had patches of old wax that blocked absorption.
attrs.fix: Wait 24 hours for the colour to even out. If patches remain, wipe the whole belt with leather cleaner, allow to dry, and reapply conditioner evenly.
